summary: Graham argues that there aren’t more Googles because investors and acquirers systematically undervalue the most innovative startups. The best founders often turn down acquisition offers, and VCs—driven by consensus, large check sizes, and limited technical understanding—are too conservative to fund truly novel ideas early enough.
key_points: Early acquisition offers are often too low; turning them down correlates with later success.
The same conservatism that makes acquirers miss deals also causes VCs to underfund bold ideas.
VCs tend to follow consensus, which systematically filters out outlier ideas with the biggest upside.
A funding gap exists between tiny seed checks and large Series A rounds, and it’s growing in value.
New investors or new VC models that make smaller, bolder bets are likely to fill the gap.
money_quotes: The reason there aren't more Googles is not that investors encourage innovative startups to sell out, but that they won't even fund them.
Any really good new idea will seem bad to most people; otherwise someone would already be doing it.
The more people who have to like a new idea, the more outliers you lose.
The exciting thing about market economies is that stupidity equals opportunity.
Startups have gotten cheaper.  That means they want less money, but also that there are more of them.
key_insight: The scarcity of “Googles” comes less from founders selling early and more from conservative capital markets that underfund bold, unconventional ideas until it’s too late.
